title: DHS Paid $170 Million to Local Police as 'Incentives' for Immigration Enforcement
detail: The Marshall Project reported on April 11, 2026 that the Department of Homeland Security had paid or earmarked more than $170 million in 'incentives' to local law enforcement agencies for locating and reporting undocumented immigrants. The payments represented a major shift in how the Trump administration funded immigration enforcement, routing money directly to local and county law enforcement agencies that agreed to cooperate with federal immigration operations — including nearly $6 million specifically for enforcing laws related to unaccompanied minors. Critics called the payments a means of creating a financial incentive structure that could distort local policing priorities, pushing law enforcement toward immigration work at the expense of public safety functions. The payments came as part of the administration's broader 'quieter' enforcement shift following the Metro Surge backlash, leveraging local officers rather than high-visibility federal operations.

media.caption: DHS paid $170M to local police for immigration enforcement incentives

title: White House Urges Republican States to Report Undocumented Immigrants in Schools, Hospitals
detail: The White House on April 11, 2026 urged Republican-led state governments to dramatically increase their immigration enforcement footprint by encouraging state employees to report undocumented immigrants encountered in schools, hospitals, and benefits programs — and to limit services to those who cannot demonstrate legal immigration status. Tennessee was cited as a model state for implementing this approach. The guidance represented an expansion of the administration's enforcement network beyond federal agencies and local police to include state social service and education workers as de facto immigration informants. Advocates warned the policy would deter vulnerable populations — including children and pregnant women — from seeking medical care or educational services, creating public health risks and humanitarian concerns.

media.caption: White House urges Republican states to enforce immigration law in schools and hospitals

title: DACA Recipients Increasingly Detained and Facing Deportation Under Trump Second Term
detail: Reporting published April 11, 2026 documented that hundreds of DACA (Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals) recipients have been detained by ICE during the Trump second term, with 21 federal court petitions filed between January 2025 and March 2026 challenging the detention of DACA recipients. DACA renewal processing times increased sharply to 2.3 months — up from 0.5 months previously — as USCIS resources were redirected to enforcement functions. While courts have repeatedly upheld DACA protections in individual cases, the volume of detentions signals a de facto administrative effort to arrest DACA holders despite the program's still-active legal status. Advocates noted that many DACA detainees face extreme difficulty obtaining release on bond, as judges weigh the government's stated intent to terminate the program. The DACA program covers approximately 530,000 active recipients.

media.caption: ICE detaining DACA recipients as Trump deportation drive intensifies

title: AG Todd Blanche's Public Comments on Abrego Garcia Could Get Criminal Case Dismissed
detail: Legal analysts and the presiding federal judge indicated on April 11, 2026 that public comments by acting Attorney General Todd Blanche about the criminal case against Kilmar Abrego Garcia could jeopardize — and potentially result in the dismissal of — the human smuggling charges the government filed against him in Tennessee. Blanche had made statements prejudging the case's outcome in public forums, prompting Abrego Garcia's defense attorneys to file a motion to dismiss on grounds of selective and vindictive prosecution. CNN reported that legal experts viewed Blanche's comments as a significant misstep that could give a federal judge sufficient basis to dismiss the charges. The criminal case had been filed by the administration after the Supreme Court ordered Abrego Garcia returned to the United States — a move widely understood as giving the government a legal pretext to justify further detention and eventual deportation.

media.caption: AG Blanche's Abrego Garcia comments could get criminal case tossed
